title: Shadow Org Analysis

content.statement: The real org chart is the one power follows.
content.question: Who at your business has more influence than their title suggests? Who has less? Where's the gap?
content.equation: SOA = Informal Influence Concentration ÷ Formal Authority Alignment
sop: List your team by formal title, then separately rank them by actual influence over decisions and morale.
Circle the names where the two rankings diverge most, that's your shadow org.
For high-influence, low-title people, give them real input on decisions in their area, formally, not just informally.
Rate Informal Influence Concentration and Formal Authority Alignment 1-10 (see calculation, page 5)
Revisit the map every 6 months, influence shifts as people prove themselves or check out.
content.explainer: Every company has a shadow org. Knowing it is how you get things done when the formal chart says no.
questions: Who at your business has more influence than their title suggests? Who has less? Where's the gap?
Whose informal approval do people quietly seek before acting, even when it's not their call?
If you routed a major decision around your actual influencers instead of the org chart, would it move faster?
content.equation_example: Rate Informal Influence Concentration 1-10 (how concentrated real influence is in a few people) and Formal Authority Alignment 1-10 (how well titles match that influence). Informal Influence Concentration 8, Formal Authority Alignment 4 → SOA = 8 ÷ 4 = 2. Higher SOA means real power is badly misaligned with the org chart; an SOA near 1 means influence and title roughly match.
content.closing_statement: Know who actually moves decisions, not just titles.

caption:
Principle Nº 23: Shadow Org Analysis

The real org chart is the one power follows. Every company has a shadow org.

Ask yourself: Who at your business has more influence than their title suggests? Who has less? Where's the gap?
